schools.summary: Henry reads the defeat as the day of recompense for Saul's spared Amalekites, the slain priests of Nob, and his pursuit of David; those who followed him in sin fall with him.
Clarke marks the continuation of ch. 29; Ellicott hears the patriot-narrator's reluctance; Keil frames the whole chapter as of a piece with Saul's rejected life, paralleled in 1 Chronicles 10.

themes: one event to righteous and wicked

schools.summary: Henry: Jonathan, innocent, falls in the family's common fate; the one exception to Eliphaz's maxim; his death completes the judgment and clears David's way so God has all the glory.
Wesley: Jonathan the fairest for the crown is removed for the exercise of David's faith and to prevent succession divisions; Abinadab is the Ishui of 14:49; Ishbosheth survives.
Ellicott (with Stanley) sketches the battle in broken snatches and points ahead to David's elegy from the Book of Jashar in 2 Samuel 1.

debates.question: Why does the innocent Jonathan die with the guilty house of Saul?

sides.position: Providence removes him to clear David's path to the throne, that God alone gets the glory and succession divisions are prevented

schools.summary: Clarke keeps 'sore wounded' (with Houbigant), since only a mortal wound explains Saul's plea to be dispatched, and rejects 'feared the archers.'
Keil, Ellicott (with Gesenius and Lange) prefer 'he was sore afraid' on lexical grounds; the arrows carry out the doom pronounced at En-dor.

debates.question: Does the verse say Saul was 'sore wounded' or 'sore afraid' of the archers?

sides.position: sore wounded — grievously hit, beyond recovery
sore afraid — greatly alarmed at the archers

schools.summary: Gill reports the Jewish tradition that the armour-bearer was Doeg the Edomite, rewarded with the office for slaying the priests of Nob.
Clarke (with Delaney) reads 'took the sword' as the armour-bearer's own — Doeg's sword that slew the priests — so both die by the weapon of their crime.
Ellicott surveys the divided verdict on Saul (Bernard/Wordsworth harsh, Josephus and the Rabbis sympathetic), harmonizes the Amalekite of 2 Samuel 1, and leaves Saul in the hands of his God.

debates.question: How does Saul's death square with the Amalekite's claim in 2 Samuel 1?

sides.position: the Amalekite's story is an invention to win a reward from David; Saul died by his own sword
the accounts can be harmonized — the Amalekite finished the mortally wounded king

debates.question: What is to be thought of Saul's eternal state?

sides.position: Saul was lost for ever; no kindly thought for him
warm sympathy for his patriotic end; leave him in the hands of his God

schools.summary: Gill: the armour-bearer saw Saul dead by his own hand, not the Amalekite's, whom the loyal servant would never have let touch his master.
Henry: an aggravating circumstance — Saul's example of self-murder drew his servant into the same sin; a leader's despair is contagious, and yet the manner of death is no measure of the soul.

themes: the contagion of a leader's sin
the manner of death no measure of the soul

schools.summary: Henry distinguishes the deaths: the sons fell honourably in battle, Saul and his armour-bearer destroyed themselves — the same day but not the same death.
Clarke and Ellicott read 'all his men' as the bodyguard/house (1 Chron. 10:6), since Ishbosheth and Abner survived; Ellicott likens the guard to the fallen at Flodden.
Gill weighs the reckonings of Saul's reign — Josephus, Eupolemus, and the forty years of Acts 13:21 — conceding the chronologers disagree.

Clarke reads a panic-stricken flight; Ellicott maps the abandoned country (Issachar, Zebulun, Naphtali, and beyond Jordan) yet limits the occupation, since Abner soon rallies Ishbosheth at Mahanaim and David guards the south.
Henry reads the ruin as the cost of rejecting God and Samuel, fulfilling the warning of 1 Sam. 12:25 and Hosea's 'I gave thee a king in mine anger.'

themes: the cost of a king like the nations

schools.summary: Henry: the pursuers came only next day, so the suicide was needless; the abuse Saul dreaded overtook him, for he who thinks to save his honour by sin will lose it.
Clarke and Keil explain the delay (battle ran till night); Ellicott notes the crown was already taken by the Amalekite, calls the savagery a reprisal for Goliath, and cites Dante on Gilboa's curse.

themes: the bodies found on the morrow

schools.summary: Henry: beheading the tallest man in Israel is a reproach to the crown; publishing the victory 'in the house of their idols' shames all who withhold the praise of their achievements from the living God.
Wesley echoes the rebuke: the head set in Dagon's temple (1 Chron. 10:10) is idols robbing the living God of His honour.
Clarke and Ellicott note the sons' heads were taken too and the trophies dispersed through Philistia; Gill places the head in Dagon's temple, perhaps at Ashdod.

themes: the living God robbed of praise

schools.summary: Gill frames the hanging of captured arms in a temple as an ancient heathen custom (Homer, Virgil), the pagan inversion of Goliath's sword laid up in the tabernacle.
Henry: Ashtaroth, the idol Israel went a whoring after, now triumphs over Israel's king — a boast God would overturn through David.
Ellicott locates the Astarte temple (Ashkelon, per Herodotus) and Beth-shan in Manasseh with its Canaanite population; Clarke notes the bodies fastened by iron hooks in the main street.

themes: the body on the wall of Beth-shan

schools.summary: Clarke names the debt: gratitude for Saul's rescue of Jabesh from Nahash the Ammonite at the start of his reign (1 Sam. 11).
Ellicott traces the long arc of a first patriotic deed bearing fruit after many years (with Aytoun's lines) and fixes the geography of Jabesh-Gilead; Gill gives the river distance.

themes: one bright thread in a dark chapter

schools.summary: Ellicott: burning a corpse was reserved for criminals (Lev. 20:14), so the Chaldee reads a burning of spices (cf. Asa, 2 Chron. 16:14); but since the text says 'they burnt them,' the putrid flesh only was burned and the bones preserved.
Clarke argues for a literal burning from necessity — burial near Beth-shan was impossible and the bodies too putrid to embalm; Wesley grounds the act in gratitude and the honour of Israel.
Henry keeps the emphasis on honour, whether a perfuming with spices or the burning of putrefying flesh.

debates.question: Did the men of Jabesh literally burn the bodies, or burn spices in their honour?

sides.position: a ceremonial burning of spices, since Israel never cremated its dead
a literal burning of the flesh from necessity, the bones preserved for burial

themes: burning not the custom of Israel

schools.summary: Gill reads burial under the tree as a parable of resurrection — as the tree revives each spring, so the dry bones at the day of judgment.
Ellicott names the tamarisk, follows the bones to Kish's sepulchre at Zelah (2 Sam. 21:12), and links the seven-day fast to the mourning for Jacob (Gen. 50:10).
Clarke, closing his commentary, denies Saul was a self-murderer — a mind deranged, and a hope of mercy; Wesley (with Henry) makes the book's last note: prefer the honour that comes from God.
Henry, sterner on the deed (a fool's death, a self-murderer), still refuses the eternal verdict — secret things belong not to us.

debates.question: Was Saul, falling on his own sword, guilty of self-murder?

sides.position: No — mortally wounded already, his act was desperation and derangement, and mercy may be hoped
It was the death of a fool and a self-murderer — yet the eternal verdict is God's, not ours

themes: honour from God, not the world
